text: Distributional semantic models capture patterns of word use from text and use this information to compute vector representations of word meanings. These models have proven to be highly effective for a large number of semantic tasks that require only a measure of word similarity. A problem with these models is that they are based solely on text, and human semantic experience is richly rooted in perception. We extend the semantic representations of words with information derived from perception (vision) to build a hybrid model that has the potential to provide a more complete semantic representation of concepts. We discuss in general terms how visual word detection can be used to enrich the semantic representations of terms that are known a priori to be visually related to certain images. We then describe a model architecture that integrates all three information sources and show via a set of experiments that the hybrid model outperforms the traditional distributional semantic model and that it captures a complementary set of semantic relationships.
